one_line_takeaway: A simulation-based adversarial minimax framework couples QKD intrusion detection directly to finite-key secret-bit retention, showing that adversarially-trained hybrid (one-class + LSTM/TCN) detectors preserve 82-92% of the honest key rate against physically-constrained time-shift, blinding, PNS, and Trojan-horse attacks at FAR=1%, versus 45-75% without detection -- but the evaluation is entirely simulation-based with no physical-hardware validation.
key_finding: At the operating point FAR=1% (Table III), the detector achieves per-attack-family AUC/miss rates at 50 km / 100 km respectively: Time-shift AUC 0.993 [+/-.003] / 0.985 [+/-.004], miss 1.6% [+/-1.0] / 3.1% [+/-1.5]; Blinding AUC 0.995 [+/-.002] / 0.992 [+/-.003], miss 0.9% [+/-0.8] / 1.7% [+/-1.0]; PNS AUC 0.980 [+/-.004] / 0.964 [+/-.006], miss 4.1% [+/-2.0] / 7.6% [+/-3.0]; THA AUC 0.968 [+/-.006] / 0.950 [+/-.007], miss 6.8% [+/-3.0] / 10.4% [+/-4.0]. Overall AUC range across conditions is 0.97-0.997 with missed-attack rates of 1-12% depending on family/distance. Without detection, adaptive attacks degrade the retained secret fraction to 60-75% of the honest rate at 50 km and 45-65% at 100 km; with detection gating key distillation at FAR~1%, the system preserves 82-92% of the honest finite-key rate, a net gain of +20-35 percentage points in usable secret bits, while discarding a median 1.2% of honest traffic (IQR 0.8-1.8%). The ablation in Table IV (50 km, averaged across attack families, FAR=1%) shows: rule-based threshold baseline AUC 0.932/miss 14.7%; + non-adversarial anomaly detection AUC 0.951/miss 11.2%; + adversarial training AUC 0.976/miss 7.0%; + temporal (LSTM/TCN) modeling AUC 0.988/miss 5.3%; + full telemetry AUC 0.991/miss 4.8% -- i.e., adversarial training alone cuts miss rate from 11.2% to 7.0%, and adding the temporal model further cuts it to 5.3%. Training dynamics: naive detector starts with miss rate ~18-24%, and the alternating minimax procedure with hard-negative mining stabilizes miss rate to ~6-9% after 5-7 adversarial rounds. Feature ablation shows the top four features (decoy-residual norms, timing skew/kurtosis, detector imbalance, double-click rate) account for ~70% of decision power (SHAP/permutation importance), and removing them raises miss rate by 5-12 percentage points. Generalization to unseen mixed-attack strategies and channel drift degrades AUC by at most 0.03 and retained-key loss by at most 5 percentage points.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (Sec. I-A, IV, V) is a simulation-driven adversarial-learning framework for intrusion detection in decoy-state BB84 QKD, cast as a two-player zero-sum minimax game between a physically constrained adversary and a hybrid neural defender. Three elements are original to this work: (1) a finite-key-aware operational loss (Eq. 8) that ties missed detections directly to the degradation of the finite-key secret fraction r (computed via three-intensity decoy bounds and entropy-accumulation/EAT penalties), rather than a generic classification loss; (2) an explicit feasibility-projection operator that bounds the emulated adversary's time-shift, detector-blinding, photon-number-splitting (PNS), and Trojan-horse (THA) attacks to hardware-realistic ranges (gate-offset bounds, safe-illumination envelopes, decoy-consistency bands, leakage-monitor limits) before they are evaluated; and (3) an alternating minimax training procedure (Algorithm 1) that co-trains a defender combining a one-class model (Isolation Forest / One-Class SVM) with a temporal model (LSTM/TCN) against a derivative-free inner-loop attacker (CMA-ES / Bayesian Optimization) that performs hard-negative mining. The paper also proposes (but does not report full quantitative results for) four auxiliary modules -- CUSUM sequential detection, distributionally robust optimization (DRO) over attack mixtures, randomized smoothing for certified robustness, and bi-level co-design of the decoy schedule -- described in Sec. V-C as architectural extensions rather than as separately evaluated contributions in the Results section.
threat_model: Adversary operates within a repeated, block-synchronous zero-sum game against a decoy-state BB84 link (Sec. III). The adversary is explicitly non-omnipotent: actions are drawn from a feasible set A = union of four attack families, each projected onto physically realistic bounds via a projection operator before being applied: (1) time-shift attacks bounded by |Δt| <= Δt_max (80-150 ps, limited by gate width/jitter); (2) detector blinding/control via bright-illumination envelope I(t) bounded by vendor-safe limit I_max and consistency with bias-current proxies; (3) photon-number-splitting (PNS) via a split fraction f_split in [0,1], constrained to keep decoy-state gains/error-gains within the epsilon_decoy confidence bands of the honest Poisson-mixture model; (4) Trojan-horse attacks (THA) parameterized by back-reflection correlation rho and return power P_ret, bounded by P_ret <= P_max and rho <= rho_max to model the sensitivity of internal leakage monitors. Trust assumptions: authenticated classical channel, private/trusted randomness for Alice/Bob's basis and intensity choices, calibrated clocks/gating within vendor spec. The work explicitly does not assume device independence -- detector-side vulnerabilities are treated as part of the attack surface -- and explicitly scopes itself to prepare-and-measure decoy-state BB84 rather than MDI-QKD (which removes detector side channels architecturally but at higher deployment cost). Within a block, defender and adversary do not observe each other's internal randomness, but both can adapt strategy across blocks based on the history of alarms.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Sec. VIII): (1) detectability drops when side-channel leakage falls below the monitor noise floor (e.g., extremely weak Trojan-horse back-reflections) or at very small block sizes (N < 5e4) where finite-key estimators loosen and variance dominates; (2) the entire evaluation is simulation-only -- no physical hardware testbed -- so real-world robustness depends on how faithfully the telemetry model matches actual device behavior, and a sim-to-real gap could reduce sensitivity; validation on experimental testbeds is left to future work. Additional limitations evident from the text itself but not flagged as such by the authors: the four 'advanced' modules (CUSUM sequential detection, DRO, randomized smoothing, bi-level decoy co-design) are described architecturally in Sec. V-C but the Results section (Sec. VII) reports no dedicated quantitative ablation or evaluation isolating their individual contribution -- Table IV's ablation covers only rule-based baseline, non-adversarial anomaly detection, adversarial training, temporal modeling, and full telemetry, not the four advanced modules. The inner-loop adversary optimizer is derivative-free (CMA-ES/Bayesian Optimization) rather than gradient-based, which the paper itself notes is necessitated by the non-differentiable simulator, but this also means the search is not guaranteed to find a true worst case within a fixed evaluation budget -- the reported miss rates are lower bounds on detectability against the adversary class the optimizer actually discovers, not a certified worst case (the certified-robustness claim via randomized smoothing is scoped only to feature noise, not to the discrete attack-family search).

datasets_used: Synthetic/simulated telemetry generated by the paper's own high-fidelity BB84 decoy-state channel simulator (no external or real-hardware dataset); corpus split 60/20/20 train/validation/test, stratified by fiber distance L in {50 km, 100 km}, block size N in {5x10^4, 2x10^5, 2x10^6}, and channel-drift settings, split by simulation seed to prevent leakage
quantitative_results: Overall detector AUC range 0.97-0.997 with miss rates 1-12% depending on attack family and distance
Retained secret fraction with detection: 82-92% of honest rate at FAR~1%, vs 60-75% (50km) / 45-65% (100km) without detection -- net gain of +20-35 percentage points
Median honest-traffic discard rate 1.2% (IQR 0.8-1.8%) at FAR=1%

Table IV ablation (50km, avg across attack families, FAR=1%): rule-based baseline AUC 0.932/miss 14.7%; +non-adversarial anomaly detection AUC 0.951/miss 11.2%; +adversarial training AUC 0.976/miss 7.0%; +temporal LSTM/TCN AUC 0.988/miss 5.3%; +full telemetry AUC 0.991/miss 4.8%
Adversarial training dynamics: naive miss rate ~18-24% at start, stabilizing to ~6-9% after 5-7 rounds of hard-negative mining

Feature ablation: removing top-4 features (decoy residuals, timing skew/kurtosis, detector imbalance, double-click rate) raises miss rate by 5-12 percentage points; these features account for ~70% of decision power
Generalization to unseen mixed attacks/drift: AUC drop <=0.03, retained-key loss <=5 percentage points
baselines_compared: Rule-based/fixed-threshold detector (Table IV baseline row: AUC 0.932, miss 14.7%)
Non-adversarial anomaly detection (trained without the inner-loop adversary; Table IV: AUC 0.951, miss 11.2%)
Non-adversarial (undefended) operation for secret-key retention comparison: retained fraction 60-75% (50km) / 45-65% (100km) vs 82-92% with the proposed adversarially-trained detector
